44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive

80	<slug>

92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116

137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)

142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143

145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.

195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

11	
12	This file is the **A.1.c.SubT** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It records the dedicated
14	substrate on which the abelian-group instance of the relative Picard quotient
15	`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` rests (the residual `sorry`
16	of `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`).
17	
18	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
19	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
20	`[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three
21	ingredients on the Lean carrier:
22	
23	1. a binary tensor-product operation
24	   `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`;
25	2. the structure sheaf `O_X` as a designated unit object for `⊗`;
26	3. an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects, i.e.
27	   the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)` of an invertible sheaf.

29	At Mathlib's pinned commit (`b80f227`), only a presheaf-level version of (1)
30	is available (`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`); (2) and (3) are present
31	as scheme-level objects, but the binary operation in (1) that ties them
32	together at the `Scheme.Modules` level is missing, and there is no
33	`MonoidalCategory` instance on `Scheme.Modules X`. This file records the
34	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) + (3)
35	into a monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.

186	/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/
187	
188	/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**
189	
190	For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
191	`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
192	product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
193	`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).
194	
195	Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
196	body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
197	`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
198	the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
199	noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
200	  ((PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).obj
201	      (PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj (R := X.presheaf) M.val N.val) :
202	    SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf)
203	
204	/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**
205	
206	A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
207	morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
208	and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
209	bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.
210	
211	Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
212	scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
213	morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
214	sheafification. -/
215	noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
216	    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
217	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).map
218	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorHom
219	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) f.val g.val)
220	
221	/-! ## §2. (Off the critical path) the full monoidal-category structure
222	
223	iter-206 PIVOT: the full `MonoidalCategory (X.Modules)` instance is
224	**deliberately not built**. Per blueprint `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path`,
225	the relative Picard group law is a structure on *isomorphism classes* of line
226	bundles — every group axiom is a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)` proposition, so no monoidal
227	coherence (pentagon/triangle/hexagon) and no `MonoidalClosed` structure is ever
228	consumed. The earlier `monoidalCategory := sorry` instance (and the two
229	localization-transport supplements `isMonoidal_W_of_whiskerLeft`,
230	`monoidalCategoryOfIsMonoidalW`) routed through the verified-absent
231	`MonoidalClosed (PresheafOfModules R₀)` wall; they are removed here. The group
232	law is built directly on the line-bundle subcategory from the four
233	existence-of-iso lemmas below, mirroring Mathlib's `CommRing.Pic`. -/
234	
235	/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)
236	
237	The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
238	locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
239	`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
240	typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/
241	
242	/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**
243	
244	A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
245	isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
246	tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
247	presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
248	`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
249	ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
250	noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
251	    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
252	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
253	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
254	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
255	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
256	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))
257	
258	/-- **The substrate tensor of the structure sheaf with itself is the structure
259	sheaf.**
260	
261	`tensorObj 𝒪_X 𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_X`, where `𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the
262	designated unit object. Built from the presheaf-level left unitor
263	`λ_ (𝟙_)` (the unit of the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal category is exactly
264	`SheafOfModules.unit.val`) under sheafification, composed with the
265	sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the (already-sheaf) unit. -/
266	noncomputable def tensorObj_unit_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} :
267	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
268	      ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
269	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
270	      (λ_ (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) ≪≫
271	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app
272	      (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
273	
274	/-- **Refining a trivialisation to a smaller open.** If `M` is trivialised on an
275	open `U` (`M.restrict U.ι ≅ 𝒪_U`), it is trivialised on every open `W ≤ U`.
276	
277	The chart-chase is identical in spirit to `LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback`:
278	factor `W.ι = (X.homOfLE hWU) ≫ U.ι`, transport through `restrictFunctorCongr`
279	and `restrictFunctorComp` to identify `M.restrict W.ι` with
280	`(M.restrict U.ι).restrict (X.homOfLE hWU)`, restrict the given trivialisation
281	`e` along that open immersion, and identify the restricted unit with the unit
282	via `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` + `pullbackObjUnitToUnit` (an isomorphism
283	because the open immersion's `Opens.map` is `Final`). -/
284	noncomputable def restrictIsoUnitOfLE {X : Scheme.{u}} {M : X.Modules} {U W : X.Opens}
285	    (hWU : W ≤ U)
286	    (e : M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf) :
287	    M.restrict W.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (W : Scheme).ringCatSheaf := by
288	  have hWU' : W ≤ (𝟙 X) ⁻¹ᵁ U := hWU
289	  set j : (W : Scheme) ⟶ (U : Scheme) := Scheme.Hom.resLE (𝟙 X) U W hWU' with hj
290	  have hjι : j ≫ U.ι = W.ι := by rw [hj, Scheme.Hom.resLE_comp_ι, Category.comp_id]
291	  haveI : (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map j.base).Final :=
292	    CategoryTheory.final_of_representablyFlat _
293	  -- M.restrict W.ι ≅ (pullback W.ι).obj M
294	  refine (Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback W.ι).app M ≪≫ ?_
295	  -- ≅ (pullback (j ≫ U.ι)).obj M
296	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hjι.symm).app M ≪≫ ?_
297	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj ((pullback U.ι).obj M)
298	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp j U.ι).symm.app M ≪≫ ?_
299	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj (M.restrict U.ι)
300	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso
301	    ((Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback U.ι).symm.app M) ≪≫ ?_
302	  -- ≅ (pullback j).obj 𝒪_U
303	  refine (Scheme.Modules.pullback j).mapIso e ≪≫ ?_
304	  -- ≅ 𝒪_W
305	  haveI hI : IsIso (SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit j.toRingCatSheafHom) := inferInstance
306	  exact @asIso _ _ _ _ _ hI
307	
308	/-- **Substrate tensor commutes with restriction along an open immersion.**
309

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). But for line bundles, the comparison map is the oplax \(\delta\) of the left
96	adjoint, produced by \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\)
97	(\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Functor}) applied to the
98	pullback--pushforward adjunction on presheaves of modules; the sole
99	project-side ingredient is the sectionwise
100	\((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}\) instance, and
101	flatness of line bundles then makes \(\delta\) an isomorphism. This is
102	exactly the carrier on which the relative Picard group law of
103	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} rests, and it is the subject of
104	\cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
105	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
106	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
107	
108	\section{Mathlib API survey}
109	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
110	
111	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
112	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
113	
114	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
115	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
116	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
117	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
118	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
119	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
120	sectionwise tensor product
121	\[
122	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
123	\]
124	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
125	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
126	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
127	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
128	
129	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
131	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
132	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
133	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
134	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
135	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
136	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
137	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
138	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
139	tensor-product operation
140	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
141	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
142	
143	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
144	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
145	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
146	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
147	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
148	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
149	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
150	\emph{flat-scoped restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
151	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
152	for line bundles \(L, M\) and the restriction along an open immersion
153	\(f\), the canonical comparison
154	\((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes M|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
155	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
156	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume; for line bundles, the comparison
157	map is the oplax \(\delta\) of \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) applied to
158	the pullback--pushforward adjunction, with the sole project-side ingredient the
159	sectionwise \((\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}\) instance,
160	and flatness of line bundles then upgrades \(\delta\) to an isomorphism
161	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
162	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
163	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
164	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
165	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
166	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
167	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
168	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
169	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
170	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
171	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
172	independently of any upstream timing.
173	
174	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
175	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
176	
177	\begin{definition}
178	
179	\leanok
180	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
181	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
182	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
183	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
184	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
185	  \[
186	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
187	  \]
188	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
189	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
190	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
191	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
192	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
193	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
194	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
195	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
196	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
197	  canonical compatibility
198	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
199	     \;\cong\;
200	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
201	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
202	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
203	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
204	
205	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
206	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
207	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
208	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
209	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
210	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
211	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
212	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
213	\end{definition}
214	
215	\begin{lemma}
216	
217	\leanok
218	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
219	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
221	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
222	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
223	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
224	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
225	  \[
226	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
227	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
228	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
229	  \]
230	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
231	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
232	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
233	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
234	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
235	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
236	  \[
237	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
238	    \qquad
239	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
240	  \]
241	  the associator
242	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
243	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
244	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
245	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
246	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
247	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
252	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
253	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
254	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
255	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
256	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
257	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
258	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
259	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
260	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
261	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
262	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
263	\end{proof}

265	\begin{remark}
266	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
267	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
268	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
269	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
270	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
271	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
272	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
273	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
274	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
275	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
276	  depend on it.
277	
278	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
279	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
280	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
281	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
282	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
283	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
284	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
285	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
286	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
287	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
288	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
289	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
290	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
291	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
292	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
293	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
294	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
295	
296	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
297	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the flat-scoped
298	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
299	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
300	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
301	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
302	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
303	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
304	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
305	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
306	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
307	\end{remark}

309	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
310	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
311	
312	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
313	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
314	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
315	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
316	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
317	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the flat-scoped restriction-compatibility
318	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
319	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
320	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
321	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
322	cover, together with their restriction to the
323	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
324	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
325	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
326	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
330	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
331	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
332	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
333	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
334	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
335	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
336	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
337	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
338	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
339	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
340	  \[
341	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
342	      \;\;:=\;\;
343	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
344	  \]
345	  is lax monoidal. This supplies the hypothesis
346	  \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\) required by the
347	  mate construction \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) in the proof of
348	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and is the \emph{sole} genuinely
349	  project-side ingredient that construction depends on.
350	\end{lemma}
351	
352	\begin{proof}
353	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
354	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
355	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
356	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
357	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
358	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
359	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
360	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
361	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
362	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
363	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
364	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
365	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
366	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
367	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
368	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
369	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
370	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
371	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
372	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
373	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
374	
375	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
376	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
377	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
378	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
379	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
380	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
381	\end{proof}
382	
383	\begin{lemma}
384	
385	\leanok
386	[Tensor product commutes with restriction for line bundles]
387	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
389	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
390	  % NOTE: the comparison map below is NOT built by hand. It is the
391	  % oplax-monoidal comparison δ of the left adjoint `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ`,
392	  % obtained from the already-present Mathlib construction
393	  % `CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal` applied to the present
394	  % adjunction `PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ`. That
395	  % construction's sole hypothesis is `[(pushforward φ).LaxMonoidal]`, and the
396	  % SOLE remaining project-side ingredient is the sectionwise lax-monoidal
397	  % instance of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. This is a bounded
398	  % `mathlib-build` target (one sectionwise lax instance, ~40--90 LOC), not a
399	  % multi-file wall: every other piece (the mate δ, the `comp`, the flat-exactness
400	  % upgrade to an iso) is already in Mathlib.
401	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be line
402	  bundles (equivalently: locally-trivial rank-one modules, hence flat), and
403	  let \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be the restriction along an open immersion.
404	  The canonical comparison morphism
405	  \[
406	    (L \otimes_X M)\big|_f
407	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
408	    L\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; M\big|_f
409	  \]
410	  is an isomorphism. The intended Lean target is
411	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
412	\end{lemma}
413	
414	\begin{proof}
415	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
416	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of (commutative) structure-sheaf-valued
417	  presheaves of rings induced by the open immersion \(f\), so that
418	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor
419	  on presheaves of modules. The proof proceeds by first \emph{constructing}
420	  the comparison morphism as the canonical base-change map of a left adjoint,
421	  and only then upgrading it to an isomorphism using flatness.
422	
423	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The
424	  restriction \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion
425	  \(f\) is naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
426	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom}).\mathrm{obj}(-)\),
427	  via the present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\).
428	  It therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism
429	  for the abstract pullback functor.
430	
431	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
432	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
433	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
434	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
435	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
436	   \;\cong\;
437	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
438	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence
439	  the whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the
440	  base-change comparison
441	  \[
442	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
443	      \;\longrightarrow\;
444	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
445	      \;\otimes\;
446	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
447	  \]
448	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\).
449	
450	  \emph{Step 3 (the comparison map is the mate of pushforward).} The functor
451	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is a left adjoint --- it is
452	  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\)
453	  through the present adjunction
454	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi
455	    : \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
456	  The base-change comparison of Step 2 is exactly the oplax-monoidal
457	  comparison \(\delta\) of this left adjoint, obtained by the mate
458	  construction \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\)
459	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Functor}), whose data are
460	  \(\delta_{A,B} = (\mathrm{adj}.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\bigl(
461	    (\eta_A \otimes \eta_B) \circ \mu_{\cdot}\bigr)\) for the unit \(\eta\) of
462	  the adjunction and the lax structure \(\mu\) of the right adjoint. The sole
463	  hypothesis of that construction is that the right adjoint be lax monoidal,
464	  i.e.\ \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\); this is
465	  furnished by \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. The comparison map is
466	  thus produced \emph{abstractly}, with no sectionwise formula required.
467	
468	  \emph{Step 4 (flatness upgrades \(\delta\) to an isomorphism).} It remains
469	  to check that, for line bundles, this already-constructed \(\delta\) is an
470	  isomorphism --- and \emph{this} is where flatness enters. The check is
471	  local, so it suffices to verify it sectionwise, where the comparison is the
472	  ordinary base-change map of modules along a ring map and \(\delta\)
473	  specialises to \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,(-)\) applied to the
474	  structural comparison for the second factor. A line bundle is locally free
475	  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
476	  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
477	  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\).
478	  Tensor is always right-exact, so \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity,
479	  and flatness of \(L\) makes \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve
480	  injectivity (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
481	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
482	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
483	  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
484	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
485	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}). We stress that this step
486	  \emph{upgrades} the comparison \(\delta\) of Step 3 to an isomorphism; it
487	  does \emph{not} construct the map, and is not a substitute for Step 3.
488	
489	  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise/affine isomorphisms of Step 4 are compatible
490	  with the restriction maps, so by \(\mathtt{hom\_ext}\) they assemble into a
491	  single isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules; transporting back through
492	  the isomorphisms of Steps 1--2 yields the asserted global isomorphism
493	  \((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes_U M|_f\).
494	\end{proof}

657	  % associativity/unit/commutativity existence-of-iso facts --- are SEPARATE
658	  % items, NOT delivered by `tensorObjOnProduct`. Each is blocked on
659	  % \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (itself blocked on
660	  % \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}).
661	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
662	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
663	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
664	  \[
665	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
666	    \;\;=\;\;
667	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
668	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
669	  \]
670	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
671	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
672	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
673	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
674	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
675	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
676	\end{lemma}
677	
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
680	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
681	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
682	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
683	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
684	  Direct from
685	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
686	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
687	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
688	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
689	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
690	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
691	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
692	  associativity, unit and commutativity isomorphisms of
693	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
694	  are isomorphisms of line bundles, hence restrict to the subtype;
695	  together with the inverse of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
696	  they equip the isomorphism classes of
697	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with the full
698	  group-law data (associativity, unit, commutativity, inverse).
699	\end{proof}

701	\begin{lemma}
702	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
703	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
704	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
705	  % NOTE: no Lean declaration yet; blocked on lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso
706	  % (which is blocked on lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal).
707	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
708	  functor
709	  \[
710	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
711	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
712	  \]
713	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
714	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
715	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
716	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
717	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
718	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
719	  canonical isomorphism
720	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
721	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
722	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
723	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
724	\end{lemma}
725	
726	\begin{proof}
727	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
728	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
729	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
730	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
731	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
732	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
733	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
734	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the same
735	  flat-exactness / restriction-compatibility argument as in
736	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (extension of scalars along \(A \to B\)
737	  is itself flat, so the comparison is an isomorphism). The compatibility
738	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
739	  same cover.
740	\end{proof}

742	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
743	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
744	
745	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
746	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
747	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
748	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
749	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
750	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
751	
752	\begin{theorem}
753	
754	\leanok
755	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
756	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
757	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
758	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
759	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
760	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
761	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
762	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
763	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
764	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
765	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
766	
767	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
768	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
769	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
770	  % groups)
771	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
772	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
773	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
774	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
775	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
776	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
777	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
778	  % \end{dfn}
779	  %  [\dots]
780	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
781	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
782	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
783	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
784	  % topologies  by
785	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
786	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
787	  %  \end{dfn}"
788	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
789	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
790	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
791	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
792	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
793	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
794	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
795	  \[
796	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
797	  \]
798	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
799	  abelian-group structure with addition
800	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
801	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
802	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
803	  inverse
804	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
805	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
806	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
807	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
808	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
809	  map
810	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
811	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
812	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
813	\end{theorem}
814	
815	\begin{proof}
816	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
817	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
818	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
819	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
820	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
821	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
822	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
823	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
824	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
825	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
826	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
827	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
828	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
829	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
830	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
831	  symmetrically in the second argument. The group axioms are then
832	  \emph{exactly} the four existence-of-isomorphism facts established above,
833	  read on isomorphism classes:
834	  \begin{itemize}
835	    \item \textbf{associativity} \(([L]+[M])+[N] = [L]+([M]+[N])\) is
836	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso};
837	    \item \textbf{neutral element} \([\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}] + [L] = [L]\)
838	      (and on the right) is \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso};
839	    \item \textbf{commutativity} \([L]+[M]=[M]+[L]\) is
840	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso};
841	    \item \textbf{inverse} \([L] + [L^{-1}] = [\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}]\),
842	      with \(L^{-1}=\mathcal{H}om(L,\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T})\), is
843	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
844	  \end{itemize}
845	  Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the existence of an isomorphism ---
846	  so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon, triangle, hexagon) is invoked or
847	  required; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is assembled
848	  directly from these four \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\)
849	  statements. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group:
850	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
851	  (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup})
852	  transported from
853	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\), the
854	  iso-class arithmetic being the family of existence-of-iso facts
855	  \([M \otimes N] = [M]\cdot[N]\) and \([M^{\vee}] = [M]^{-1}\); here the same
856	  shape is transported to the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory
857	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). (The scheme-level Picard
858	  group is genuinely absent from Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}
859	  records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project
860	  supplies it lightly.) The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's
861	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), mirroring Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)
862	  verbatim.
863	
864	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
865	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
866	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
867	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
868	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
869	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
870	
871	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
872	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
873	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
874	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
875	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
876	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
877	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
878	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
879	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
880	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
881	\end{proof}
